questions.question: You feed a 224×224 RGB image to a conv with kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=0. What is the output spatial size?
questions.explanation: Output size = floor((H - K + 2P) / S) + 1 = floor((224 - 3 + 0) / 1) + 1 = 222. Without padding, every 3×3 convolution shrinks the feature map by 2 pixels on each axis — one pixel off each border. Memorise this formula; you will compute it dozens of times per architecture.

questions.question: `pad2d(x, p)` uses `x.shape[:-2]` when computing the output shape. For input `x` of shape `(C, H, W)` and `p=1`, what is the shape of the returned array?
options: (C, H+1, W+1) — one strip added per side
(C+1, H+2, W+2) — all axes including channels are padded
(C, H+2, W+2) — padding of p=1 adds one row/column on each side, so height and width both grow by 2
(C, H+1, W) — only the vertical axis is padded
questions.explanation: pad2d adds p zeros to each of the four borders (top, bottom, left, right) of the spatial dimensions. Height grows from H to H+2p and width from W to W+2p. The `x.shape[:-2]` trick propagates the leading axes (C, or N,C) unchanged so the same function handles 2D, 3D, and 4D tensors without modification.

questions.question: A 32×32 input is convolved with kernel_size=3, padding=1, stride=2. Applying the formula `floor((H - K + 2P) / S) + 1`, what is the output spatial size?
options: 32 — same-padding with stride 2 preserves spatial size
15 — computing floor((32-3+2)/2) and forgetting the +1
17 — incorrectly computing (32-3+2)/2 + 1 with wrong precedence
questions.explanation: Plugging in: floor((32 - 3 + 2) / 2) + 1 = floor(31/2) + 1 = 15 + 1 = 16. Stride 2 with P=1 on a 32×32 input halves the spatial dimensions. This is the standard 'downsample-by-2' recipe used throughout ResNet and ConvNeXt.

questions.question: Inside `conv2d_naive`, the line `out[oc, i, j] = np.sum(patch * w[oc])` where both `patch` and `w[oc]` have shape `(C_in, kh, kw)`. What arithmetic operation does this represent?
options: The dot product (sum of element-wise products) across all input channels and kernel positions — producing one scalar, the activation for output channel `oc` at position `(i, j)`
The maximum value of the element-wise product across all kernel positions for that output channel
The L2 norm of the difference between the input patch and the kernel
The sum of only the centre element of each kernel slice, ignoring the surrounding weights
questions.explanation: `np.sum(patch * w[oc])` first multiplies the two tensors element-wise (C_in × kh × kw multiplications) then sums all values to a single scalar. This is the dot product of two flattened vectors of length C_in×kh×kw — the mathematically correct definition of one convolutional output pixel.

questions.question: What does the `im2col` transformation actually do?
options: It compresses the image to fit in GPU cache
It extracts every kernel-sized receptive window from the input and stacks them as columns so that convolution becomes a single matrix multiply
It converts float32 pixels to int8 for faster arithmetic
It concatenates all images in a batch into a single 2D matrix
questions.explanation: im2col reshapes the input so that each column is one flattened receptive field of size C_in×kh×kw. Flattening the kernels into rows reduces convolution to a single GEMM call (w_flat @ cols), which GPUs execute orders of magnitude faster than a Python quadruple loop. Every production conv library — cuDNN, oneDNN, MLX — is some variant of this.

questions.question: You stack four 3×3 convolutions (all stride 1, no pooling). What is the receptive field of a neuron in the final layer?
options: 3×3 — each conv is 3×3 and depth does not compound
5×5 — two convs give 5×5 and additional depth adds nothing
9×9 — each conv adds 2 to each side: RF = 1 + L×(K-1) = 1 + 4×2 = 9
15×15 — every conv triples the receptive field
questions.explanation: For L stacked K×K convs with stride 1, RF = 1 + L×(K-1). With L=4 and K=3 that is 1 + 4×2 = 9. The same formula generalises with stride: receptive field grows multiplicatively through strided layers, which is why a deep network with downsampling can cover the whole image in ten or fifteen blocks.
